title: The Mechanical Design Process
delivery: SERVER-SIDE ONLY. This file, and especially every answer index, must never be sent to the client. The server selects and shuffles items, sends only stems and options, and grades submissions server-side.

objectives.demand-vs-goal: Classify a requirement as a demand (pass or fail) or a goal (preferred).
objectives.sequence: Know the order of the design-process steps.
objectives.next-step: Given a situation, identify the correct next step.
objectives.constraints-first: Understand why constraints are set before geometry.
objectives.select-why: Understand selecting with a documented reason.
objectives.prototype-purpose: Understand what a prototype is for.
objectives.iteration: Understand that the first version is never the last.
objectives.scenario-apply: Apply the whole process to a described situation.

items.stem: The intake must fit within the frame perimeter or the robot is illegal. Is this a demand or a goal?
options: A demand, because it must be true or the design fails
A goal, because smaller is preferred
Neither, because it depends on the game
A goal, because size is flexible

items.stem: We would like the arm to weigh under three pounds if we can manage it. Is this a demand or a goal?
options: A constraint that cannot be classified

items.stem: The climber must hold the robot's full weight at the end of the match. Demand or goal?
options: A goal, because it is only at the end

items.stem: Ideally the shooter reaches the far zone, but hitting the near zone is acceptable. Demand or goal?
Which of these is a goal rather than a demand?
options: The gearbox must bolt to the existing frame rail
The mechanism must fit in the allowed volume
It would be nice if the mechanism were under four ounces
The part must not break under match loads

items.stem: A demand is usually written with the word must. Goals are often signaled by which kind of wording?
options: None, goals are not written down

items.stem: Why does separating demands from goals matter before you design?
options: It does not matter, both are optional
Demands are pass or fail while goals only decide between options that already pass
Goals are more important than demands
Demands can be ignored if goals are met

items.stem: In the design process, which happens first?
Which step immediately follows generating concepts?
The design process ends with which activity?
Which step comes immediately before detailing the design in CAD?
Where does defining the problem fall in the process?
options: Last, once the part is built

items.stem: Which ordering reflects the design process?
options: Define, generate concepts, prototype, select, detail, make, test

items.stem: You have three sketched bracket concepts and no data on which one works. What is the next step?
options: Detail the best-looking one in CAD
Build a quick prototype to test the concepts
Order the material for all three
Pick one at random and manufacture it

items.stem: Your cardboard mockup answered its question and one concept clearly won. What is next?
options: Select that concept, record why, then detail it

items.stem: You have written the one-sentence job and listed the constraints. What is next?
A concept is selected and the reason is documented. What is the next step?
options: Detail the design into a clean CAD model

items.stem: You have finished a clean CAD model of the chosen bracket. What is next?
options: Manufacture it and then test it

items.stem: Why set constraints before sketching geometry?
options: To make the drawing look neater
Constraints shrink the design space so the geometry has something to satisfy
To slow the process down on purpose

items.stem: A designer opens CAD and starts extruding with no constraints defined. What is the main risk?
options: The file will be too large
The part may not fit, mount, or meet weight because nothing bounded the design
Nothing, this is the correct approach

items.stem: Which of these is a real constraint to set before designing an intake mount?
options: The space available and the tube it bolts to

items.stem: Why document the reason a concept was chosen?
options: To fill pages in the notebook
It records design intent and stops the team from re-arguing the decision
It is required by the game manual

items.stem: Two concepts both meet every demand. What decides between them?
Where is the reasoning behind a design choice recorded?
What is the purpose of a crude prototype?
options: To be the final competition part
To answer a specific question and test an assumption

items.stem: A team spends a week building a beautiful, final-quality prototype before testing the concept at all. What is wrong?
options: Nothing, more polish is always better
A prototype should be fast and crude to answer a question, so polishing before proving the concept wastes time
The prototype should have been skipped entirely
They should have manufactured the real part first

items.stem: A good early prototype is best described as which of these?
options: Fast, crude, and focused on one question

items.stem: Your printed bracket works but the bearing bore came out loose. What does the design process say to do?
options: Tighten the dimension and reprint, since iteration is expected
Blame the printer and move on

items.stem: The idea that the first version is never the last means what?
options: You should only ever build one version
Expect to test, learn, and improve rather than treating version one as final
The first version is always a failure
Iteration is a waste of time

items.stem: A mechanism fails its first test. What is the healthy response within the design process?
options: Analyze what failed, improve the design, and iterate
Keep the design unchanged and hope

items.stem: A team jumps straight from defining the problem to detailing the design in CAD, skipping concepts and prototyping. What is the most likely consequence?
options: They save time with no downside
They commit to an unproven idea and may waste manufacturing time on a design that does not work
The robot will automatically be better

items.stem: Which situation best shows the design process working rather than failing?
options: A part is built once and never revisited
A prototype reveals a flaw, the design is changed, and the next version is better
A concept is chosen with no reason recorded
CAD is started before any constraints exist
